annotations.label: Category (informal)

anchor.snippet: is a collection of "objects" that are linked by "arrows"

annotations.note: `Category` is a typeclass of objects with morphisms (arrows), composition, and identities.

annotations.label: Functor

anchor.snippet: A mapping between two categories compatible with their respective structures is called a functor

annotations.note: `Functor C D` is a structure-preserving map between categories, with notation `C ⥤ D`.

annotations.label: Definition of a category

anchor.snippet: One commonly used definition is as follows

annotations.note: `Category` (extending `CategoryStruct`) is exactly this standard definition with hom-types, identities, composition and axioms.

annotations.label: Category axioms (associativity and units)

anchor.snippet: such that the following axioms hold

annotations.note: The fields `id_comp`, `comp_id`, and `assoc` of `Category` are precisely the unit and associativity axioms.

annotations.label: Hom-class notation

anchor.snippet: to denote the hom-class of all morphisms from

annotations.note: Morphisms from `X` to `Y` form the type `X ⟶ Y` (`Quiver.Hom`), the formal counterpart of the hom-class notation.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of identity morphism

anchor.snippet: one can prove that there is exactly one identity morphism for every object

annotations.note: Identities are bundled as data, but `id_of_comp_left_id` proves any morphism acting as a (left) identity equals `𝟙 X`, capturing uniqueness.

annotations.label: Object-free definition of categories

anchor.snippet: categories can be defined without reference to objects at all

annotations.note: Mathlib defines categories via a type of objects (`Quiver`/`Category`) and has no arrows-only/object-free formulation.

annotations.label: Small and large categories

anchor.snippet: is called small if both

annotations.note: `SmallCategory` (objects and morphisms in the same universe) and `LargeCategory` formalize the small/large distinction.

annotations.label: Locally small category

anchor.snippet: A locally small category is a category such that for all objects

annotations.note: `LocallySmall.{w} C` asserts every hom-type `X ⟶ Y` is `w`-small.

annotations.label: Category of sets (Set)

anchor.snippet: forms a large category, Set

annotations.note: The instance `CategoryTheory.types : Category (Type u)` makes sets and functions a (large) category.

annotations.label: Category Rel

anchor.snippet: The category Rel consists of all sets (as objects) with binary relations between them

annotations.note: `RelCat` is the large category of types with binary relations as morphisms.

annotations.label: Discrete category

anchor.snippet: Such categories are called discrete

annotations.note: `Discrete α` carries the discrete `SmallCategory` whose only morphisms are identities.

annotations.label: Preordered set as a category

anchor.snippet: forms a small category, where the objects are the members of

annotations.note: Any preorder is a `SmallCategory` with a unique morphism `X ⟶ Y` iff `X ≤ Y`.

annotations.label: Thin category

anchor.snippet: such a category is called thin

annotations.note: `Quiver.IsThin` states every hom-type is a `Subsingleton`, i.e. at most one morphism between any two objects.

annotations.label: Monoid as a category

anchor.snippet: forms a small category with a single object

annotations.note: `SingleObj M` is the one-object category whose endomorphism monoid recovers the monoid `M`.

annotations.label: Group as a category

anchor.snippet: can be seen as a category with a single object in which every morphism is invertible

annotations.note: When `M` is a group, `SingleObj M` is the one-object groupoid in which every morphism is invertible.

annotations.label: Isomorphism

anchor.snippet: A morphism that is invertible in this sense is called an isomorphism

annotations.note: `Iso X Y` (notation `X ≅ Y`) bundles a morphism with a two-sided inverse; `IsIso` is the unbundled predicate.

annotations.label: Groupoid

anchor.snippet: A groupoid is a category in which every morphism is an isomorphism

annotations.note: `Groupoid` extends `Category` with an inverse for every morphism.

annotations.label: Fundamental groupoid

anchor.snippet: called the fundamental groupoid of

annotations.note: `FundamentalGroupoid X` has a `Groupoid` instance with homotopy classes of paths as morphisms.

annotations.label: Free category generated by a graph

anchor.snippet: is called the free category generated by the graph

annotations.note: `Paths V` is the path/free category generated by a quiver (directed graph), with the universal `lift` property.

annotations.label: Category Ord

anchor.snippet: forms a category, Ord

annotations.note: `Preord` is the category of preordered sets with monotone maps, matching Ord.

annotations.label: Concrete category

anchor.snippet: i.e. a category whose objects can be identified with sets with some type of additional structure

annotations.note: `ConcreteCategory` equips a category with a faithful forgetful functor to types.

annotations.label: Category of groups (Grp)

anchor.snippet: forms a large category, Grp

annotations.note: `GrpCat` is the bundled large category of groups and group homomorphisms.

annotations.label: Category Ab

anchor.snippet: consisting of all abelian groups and their group homomorphisms

annotations.note: `Ab` is an abbreviation for `AddCommGrpCat`, the category of abelian groups and homomorphisms.

annotations.label: Category of graphs

anchor.snippet: The class of all graphs forms another concrete category

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Quiv`, the category of quivers (directed multigraphs) with prefunctors, but no category of simple graphs.

annotations.label: Fiber bundles as a concrete category

anchor.snippet: Fiber bundles with bundle maps between them form a concrete category

annotations.note: Fiber bundles exist in Mathlib but are not organized into a category of bundles with bundle maps.

annotations.label: Category Cat

anchor.snippet: The category Cat consists of all small categories, with functors between them as morphisms

annotations.note: `Cat` is the large category of small categories with functors as morphisms.

annotations.label: Functor category

anchor.snippet: a functor category has as objects functors between two fixed categories

annotations.note: `Functor.category` is the category `C ⥤ D` with natural transformations as morphisms.

annotations.label: Dual (opposite) category

anchor.snippet: This is called the dual or opposite category

annotations.note: `Category.opposite` provides the category structure on `Cᵒᵖ` by reversing morphisms.

annotations.label: Product category

anchor.snippet: one can form the product category

annotations.note: `CategoryTheory.prod` gives the Cartesian product category structure on `C × D`.

annotations.label: Quotient category

anchor.snippet: one can construct the quotient category

annotations.note: `Quotient r` is the category obtained by quotienting hom-sets by a `HomRel`.

annotations.label: Overcategory (slice category)

anchor.snippet: the overcategory or slice category

annotations.note: `Over X` is the slice/over category of objects equipped with a morphism to `X`.

annotations.label: Undercategory and comma category

anchor.snippet: The dual notion is that of an undercategory or coslice category

annotations.note: `Under X` is the coslice category, and the general `Comma L R` construction is also formalized.

annotations.label: Localization

anchor.snippet: turns the morphisms from

annotations.note: `Localization W` constructs the category where morphisms in `W` become isomorphisms, with the `IsLocalization` predicate characterizing it.

annotations.label: Monomorphism

anchor.snippet: a monomorphism (or monic ) if it is left-cancellable

annotations.note: `Mono f` is the left-cancellation (postcomposition) property defining monomorphisms.

annotations.label: Epimorphism

anchor.snippet: an epimorphism (or epic ) if it is right-cancellable

annotations.note: `Epi f` is the right-cancellation (precomposition) property defining epimorphisms.

annotations.label: Bimorphism

anchor.snippet: a bimorphism if it is both a monomorphism and an epimorphism

annotations.note: Both `Mono` and `Epi` exist, but there is no bundled `Bimorphism` definition for their conjunction.

annotations.label: Retraction

anchor.snippet: a retraction if it has a right inverse

annotations.note: `IsSplitEpi f` (via `SplitEpi`, with `section_ ≫ f = 𝟙`) is the property of having a right inverse.

annotations.label: Section

anchor.snippet: a section if it has a left inverse

annotations.note: `IsSplitMono f` (via `SplitMono`, with `f ≫ retraction = 𝟙`) is the property of having a left inverse.

annotations.label: Isomorphism (morphism)

anchor.snippet: an isomorphism if it has an inverse

annotations.note: `IsIso f` asserts existence of a two-sided inverse; `Iso` is the bundled form.

annotations.label: Endomorphism

anchor.snippet: an endomorphism if

annotations.note: `End X := X ⟶ X` is the type of endomorphisms of an object.

annotations.label: Endomorphisms form a monoid

anchor.snippet: is a set and forms a monoid under morphism composition

annotations.note: `End.monoid` provides the `Monoid (End X)` instance under composition.

annotations.label: Automorphism

anchor.snippet: is both an endomorphism and an isomorphism

annotations.note: `Aut X := X ≅ X` is the type of automorphisms (self-isomorphisms) of `X`.

annotations.label: Automorphisms form a group

anchor.snippet: it forms a group under morphism composition called the automorphism group of

annotations.note: `Aut X` carries a `Group` instance under composition of isomorphisms.

annotations.label: Retractions are epimorphisms, sections are monomorphisms

anchor.snippet: Every retraction is an epimorphism

annotations.note: `IsSplitEpi.epi` shows split epis are epis, dually `IsSplitMono.mono` shows split monos are monos.

annotations.label: Equivalence of mono+retraction, epi+section, isomorphism

anchor.snippet: The following three statements are equivalent

annotations.note: The individual implications (`isIso_of_mono_of_isSplitEpi`, `IsSplitEpi.of_iso`, etc.) are present but not packaged as one three-way equivalence.

annotations.label: Commutative diagram

anchor.snippet: can most conveniently be represented with commutative diagrams

annotations.note: `CommSq` formalizes commutative squares, but there is no general object for arbitrary commutative diagrams (which are just stated as equalities of composites).

annotations.label: Preadditive category

anchor.snippet: Such a category is called preadditive

annotations.note: `Preadditive` makes each hom-set an abelian group with bilinear composition.

annotations.label: Additive category

anchor.snippet: it is called an additive category

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Preadditive` plus biproduct classes (`HasBinaryBiproducts`, zero object) but no single bundled `AdditiveCategory` class.

annotations.label: Abelian category

anchor.snippet: then we speak of an abelian category

annotations.note: `Abelian` extends `Preadditive` with normal monos/epis, kernels and cokernels.

annotations.label: Abelian groups as an abelian category

anchor.snippet: A typical example of an abelian category is the category of abelian groups

annotations.note: The instance `Abelian AddCommGrpCat` establishes that the category of abelian groups is abelian.

annotations.label: Enriched category

anchor.snippet: can be formalized in the concept of an enriched category

annotations.note: `EnrichedCategory V C` formalizes categories whose hom-objects live in a monoidal category `V`.

annotations.label: Complete category

anchor.snippet: A category is called complete if all small limits exist in it

annotations.note: `HasLimits` (i.e. `HasLimitsOfSize.{v,v}`) asserts all small limits exist, the completeness condition.

annotations.label: Complete categories examples

anchor.snippet: The categories of sets, abelian groups and topological spaces are complete

annotations.note: `Type` has a `HasLimitsOfSize` instance, and analogous `HasLimits` instances exist for `AddCommGrp` and `TopCat`.

annotations.label: Cartesian closed category

anchor.snippet: A category is called cartesian closed if it has finite direct products

annotations.note: `CartesianClosed` is now an alias for `MonoidalClosed` with respect to the cartesian monoidal structure.

annotations.label: Cartesian closed examples

anchor.snippet: the category of complete partial orders with Scott-continuous functions

annotations.note: `Type` (and functor categories into `Type`) have cartesian-closed instances, but the cited ωCPO/Scott-continuous example is not formalized as a cartesian closed category.

annotations.label: Topos

anchor.snippet: A topos is a certain type of cartesian closed category

annotations.note: Subobject classifiers (`Classifier`/`HasClassifier`) and the ingredients of an elementary topos exist, but there is no bundled `Topos` class.
